story:
Over a single, breath-held three minutes, two worlds braid tight: Lunvéra, shrine of shadowed waters, and a New Orleans backyard where a planter holds Grandma Zora’s ashes. Brown Bondye—the old garden gnome Mr. Bo—stands watch as Soul Veil’s guard. Outside his grove the world’s undone, thrones broke, winds wail; in here his duty holds. Across a thin divide crouches the Caged Ravager, brindled muscle, storm and spit—Rex, the pit bull—eyes cold, jaw drip, seeing the Watcher as threat, the Watcher seeing him the same. Between them hums a wire of hate and oath.

Then the Royal One arrives: Princess Nia, four years bold, curls like a night-crown, carrying a morning gift—a plastic pink teapot of grape-purple libation. She pours sweet nectar at the Watcher’s feet, a child’s ceremony bright as dawn. Music thrums from Daddy’s phone: first gentle comfort, then a peppy beat, winds wild enough to thin the Veil. Nia hoists Mr. Bo high and twirls, laughing bells into the air. The libation sways him, her grip rights him, the song lifts them both.

The Ravager sees danger, hears the loud spirit-call, and the twig-thin wall—an old latch on a backyard gate—gives. He bursts free. To him, Little Cub must be guarded; to him, the Watcher’s clutch is a threat. He lunges, teeth flashing. To the Watcher, a tempest tears: he plants himself between beast and Royal One, eyes gold with Old Mother’s wrath. They grapple; jaws clamp his carved forearm; wood cracks. Snap—the arm is torn free. Pain rings the shrine.

But the Watcher is old craft. He summons her fury—the earth-mother rage Zora once woke—cursing flesh and fiber. In the Ravager’s maw, the prize sours: rot, rancid, poison-sting. The dog spits, gagging, glaring back unbowed. The prison re-forms; the span holds again. Oaths are carved on air. Watcher and Ravager lock eyes, still at war.

Now the same moment in daylight words. Nia skips out for a tea party with Mr. Bo, the faded brown gnome her Great-Gran Zora called Brown Bondye. She clinks cups, whispers that he watches them. Daddy—Jamal—tends bulbs in a redwood box, Grandma Zora mixed with soil like she asked: Haiti in her blood, N’awlins in her bones. He’s wary of Vodou talk, calls it crazy, but keeps her marker close. The music shuffles to “Happy,” and Nia spins Bo like a partner. The gate latch she barely clicked slips. Rex rockets out, mouth eager—it’s play to him. He and Nia tug; she won’t let him have her little brown man. The arm snaps. She cries big tears. Rex drops the chewed limb, stomach turning. Daddy strides back, gathers Nia, takes Mr. Bo gentle, promises to fix him.

Jamal notes oddness: Bo’s legs sit crooked, the wood hot in April shade. Zora’s warnings scratch his mind: He watches. He was woken. He shakes it off, corrals Rex, sets Bo atop the flower mound, a one-armed sentinel at the edge of earth and ash. In that held breath where magic and ordinary overlap, the Watcher and the Ravager stare each other down, the Royal One safe in her father’s arms, the Gardener halfway between doubt and belief. The Veil settles, yet the watch goes on.
